questions.question: What is the evidence grade for exercise as a sleep intervention, based on the 2025-2026 meta-analyses?

options.text: Exercise has no effect on sleep quality
STRONG evidence — 2025 meta-analysis (81 RCTs, n=6,193) showed significant sleep quality improvements (MD = -1.77 PSQI); 2026 analysis found moderate-to-large effect (g = -0.65) for insomnia
Only medication works for sleep problems

questions.explanation: Exercise has STRONG evidence as a non-pharmacological sleep intervention. A 2025 meta-analysis of 81 RCTs (n=6,193) reported significant PSQI score reductions (MD = -1.77), and a 2026 meta-analysis focusing on moderate-intensity exercise in adults with insomnia found a clinically meaningful moderate effect (Hedges' g = -0.65). This qualifies exercise as one of the most effective non-drug sleep interventions available.

questions.question: What are the three key mechanisms by which exercise improves sleep?

options.text: Exercise only affects sleep through exhaustion

Three mechanisms: (1) body temperature regulation (core temp drops 4-6 hours post-exercise, signaling sleep onset), (2) adenosine homeostasis (builds sleep pressure), (3) anxiety reduction + parasympathetic activation
Exercise has no known mechanisms for sleep improvement
Exercise works through placebo effect only

questions.explanation: Three converging mechanisms: (1) Body temperature regulation — exercise raises core temp, then 4-6 hours later it drops below baseline (rebound hypothermia), signaling sleep onset. (2) Adenosine homeostasis — exercise increases adenosine accumulation (sleep pressure) throughout the day. (3) Anxiety reduction + parasympathetic activation — exercise reduces cortisol and sympathetic activity, shifts to rest-and-digest mode. These work together to improve sleep onset, depth, and efficiency.

questions.question: What is the optimal timing for exercise to maximize sleep benefits?

options.text: Vigorous exercise right before bed is ideal

Late afternoon/early evening (4-7 PM) is ideal for most people — allows body temperature to drop at bedtime; morning also works for circadian alignment; avoid vigorous exercise within 1-2 hours of bed
Exercise timing doesn't matter at all for sleep

questions.explanation: Late afternoon/early evening (4-7 PM) is optimal for most people because body temperature drops 4-6 hours post-exercise, aligning with bedtime. Morning exercise works well for circadian alignment (light exposure + movement strengthens rhythm). Avoid vigorous exercise within 1-2 hours of bedtime — it elevates core temperature, heart rate, and cortisol, which can delay sleep onset. Gentle movement (yoga, stretching, slow walk) close to bedtime is fine.

questions.question: Which exercise modality showed the strongest evidence for older adults with insomnia in the research?

options.text: Only HIIT works for sleep in older adults
Resistance training (strength training) showed particularly strong evidence for older adults (60+) with insomnia — outperformed aerobic in this population
Exercise has no effect on sleep in older adults

questions.explanation: The lesson highlights that resistance training is especially effective for older adults with insomnia — it outperformed aerobic exercise in this population. Why: age-related sleep changes include reduced slow-wave (deep) sleep and more fragmented sleep; resistance training helps counteract this. Recommendation: 2-3 days/week resistance training + daily walking or yoga for optimal sleep benefit in older adults.

questions.question: Based on your current sleep challenges and schedule, what's one change you could make to your exercise timing or type to improve your sleep? (Examples: switch from evening to afternoon exercise, add 10 minutes of evening yoga, go for a morning walk in sunlight, etc.)
questions.explanation: This reflection applies the lesson's timing and modality recommendations to your situation. Consider: If you exercise late evening and struggle to fall asleep → shift to 4-7 PM or morning. If you have anxiety-related sleep issues → add gentle yoga/stretching 1-2 hours before bed (parasympathetic activation). If you're an older adult with insomnia → consider adding 2-3x/week resistance training. If you're sedentary and poor sleep → start with morning walks (light exposure + circadian alignment + adenosine building).
